metadata.title: The Roofline: Is Your Model Compute- or Memory-Bound?

beats.narration_text: This is Kore for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · The Roofline: Is Your Model Compute- or Memory-Bound?

beats.narration_text: Hello. This is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. Two models with the same FLOP count run at wildly different speeds on the same chip. The roofline tells you which one the memory bus is strangling.

props.command: claude "write roofline.py: given a target's peak GFLOP/s and memory bandwidth, draw the roofline. Place a model at its arithmetic intensity (MACs/bytes moved) and label compute-bound vs memory-bound."
props.segment: The Roofline: Is Your Model Compute- or Memory-Bound?

beats.narration_text: Here is why The Roofline: Is Your Model Compute- or Memory-Bound? belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: claude "write roofline.py: given a target's peak GFLOP/s and memory bandwidth, draw the roofline. Place a model at its arithmetic intensity (MACs/bytes moved) and label compute-bound vs memory-bound."
props.segment: The Roofline: Is Your Model Compute- or Memory-Bound?

beats.narration_text: From the book: the roofline model — arithmetic intensity on x, achievable performance on y. Our script computes where your model sits on this plot and tells you which roof you're hitting.

beats.act: MANIM — Roofline axes: memory slope and compute ceiling
beats.narration_text: The roofline has two axes: arithmetic intensity on x — floating-point operations per byte moved — and attainable GFLOP per second on y. A memory-bandwidth slope rises from the origin. A compute ceiling caps it horizontally. The ridge point is where they meet.

Place the model's operating point. Arithmetic intensity of 0.8 floating-point operations per byte — left of the ridge. The model is memory-bound. More compute cores won't help. The memory bus is the bottleneck. Quantization, weight caching, and tiling are what move the needle.

beats.act: MANIM — int8 version shifts right toward ridge
beats.narration_text: Quantize to INT8. Weight bytes shrink by a factor of 4. For the same computation, fewer bytes move — arithmetic intensity rises. The operating point shifts right. The model approaches the ridge. Now memory bandwidth is less of a constraint.

Your turn. Ask Claude: write roofline.py — given a target's peak GFLOP per second and memory bandwidth, draw the roofline. Place a model at its arithmetic intensity — MACs divided by bytes moved — and label it compute-bound or memory-bound. Once you see where your model lands, you know which optimization to pursue. If it's memory-bound, quantization and caching help more than a faster compute core. Add a second point for the INT8 version and see the shift.

props.command: claude "write roofline.py: given a target's peak GFLOP/s and memory bandwidth, draw the roofline. Place a model at its arithmetic intensity (MACs/bytes moved) and label compute-bound vs memory-bound."

beats.narration_text: Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. From Humanitarians AI.
